title: Your First German Sounds
description: German sounds different — but there are rules. Learn them once and every word becomes pronounceable.

lessons.title: The German Alphabet
lessons.subtitle: 26 letters + 4 special characters. You already know 20.
lessons.hook: Here's something Duolingo never tells you: German spelling is almost perfectly phonetic. Once you know the sounds, you can read ANY German word out loud — even words you've never seen. English can't say that.
lessons.explain: German has consistent pronunciation rules. Unlike English where 'ough' is pronounced differently in 'though', 'through', 'rough', and 'thought' — in German, each letter combination always sounds the same. This means you only need to learn the rules once.

vocabulary.memoryHook: The 'tsch' sound is like 'ch' in 'church'. Tsch-üss.
Sounds like 'nine' — remember: nine letters in 'nein danke'
Bitte means BOTH please AND you're welcome — context tells you which

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Hello' in German?
exercises.explanation: Hallo is the standard informal greeting in German, used throughout the day.

exercises.prompt: Someone gives you a gift. You say 'Danke'. They reply ___
exercises.explanation: Bitte serves double duty: 'please' when asking, and 'you're welcome' when responding to thanks. Context always makes it clear.
Nein (no) + danke (thank you). The comma and period matter — German uses them the same way as English here.

lessons.title: Special German Sounds: Ä, Ö, Ü, ß
lessons.subtitle: The 4 sounds that make German unmistakably German.
lessons.hook: These 4 characters terrify beginners. They shouldn't. Each one has a very simple pattern — and native German children learn them by age 4.
lessons.explain: Ä, Ö, Ü are called 'Umlauts' — they're just vowels with a twist. The two dots (¨) mean: round your lips and tighten the sound. ß (Eszett) is simply a double-s, used after long vowels. Once you train your mouth, these become second nature within days.

breakdown.role: The ü sound — like French 'tu', lips rounded, say 'ee'

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Mäd-chen: the ä sounds like the 'e' in 'bed'
vocabulary.memoryHook: ä = 'e' in bed. Mädchen, Bäcker, Väter
Ö = round lips + say 'e'. Like you're surprised and snobbish.

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Round your lips as if to say 'oo', then say 'ee'
vocabulary.memoryHook: Ü = lips like 'oo', sound like 'ee'. Tschüss, über, grün

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: Stra-sse: sharp ss after a long vowel
vocabulary.memoryHook: ß only appears after LONG vowels (Straße) or diphthongs. After short vowels: ss (Wasser).

exercises.prompt: The word 'schön' (beautiful) — the ö sounds like:
exercises.explanation: Ö is made by rounding your lips slightly and producing the 'e' sound. Think of the British pronunciation of 'her' without the r-sound at the end.

exercises.prompt: Which spelling is correct for the German word for 'street'?
options: Straße or Strasse — both are correct
exercises.correctAnswer: Straße or Strasse — both are correct
exercises.explanation: In modern German, both Straße and Strasse are accepted. Switzerland actually dropped ß entirely and always writes ss. In Germany, ß is used after long vowels and diphthongs.
